prompts.title: DSA Problem Walkthrough With Multiple Approaches
prompts.prompt: Act as a senior DSA mentor with 10 years of experience coaching students at IIT and NIT for placements at Amazon, Microsoft, and Google India. I need you to solve a data structures problem with full pedagogical breakdown. Here is the context: I am a 3rd-year CS student at an AKTU/Anna University college preparing for campus placements and LeetCode contests. I will paste the exact problem statement (with constraints and sample I/O) right after this prompt. Please provide: (1) A 3-line restatement of the problem in plain English so I confirm I understood it, (2) Brute-force approach with time/space complexity and why it fails the constraints, (3) Better approach using a standard DSA pattern (sliding window, two pointers, DP, BFS/DFS, heap, etc.) naming the pattern explicitly, (4) Optimal approach with clean C++ and Java code, fully commented, following competitive-programming idioms (using vector, unordered_map, long long where needed), (5) Dry run on the sample input showing variable state at each step, (6) Edge cases I must handle (empty input, overflow, single element, duplicates), (7) 3 similar LeetCode/GFG problems to practice the same pattern. Format as markdown with clear H2 sections and fenced code blocks. Consider these constraints: code must compile on GCC 11 and Java 17 used in most Indian college judges, avoid Python-only tricks, and assume I know up to graph algorithms but am still learning DP. Problem statement:

prompts.title: TCS NQT and Infosys Coding Round Strategy
prompts.prompt: Act as a placement coach with 8 years of experience preparing Tier-2 and Tier-3 Indian engineering students for TCS NQT, Infosys SP/DSE, Wipro Elite, and Cognizant GenC coding rounds. I need you to build me a 30-day coding-round preparation plan. Here is the context: I am a final-year B.Tech CS student at a VTU-affiliated college in Bangalore, comfortable with C and basic Java, weak in strings and recursion, and I have placements starting in 6 weeks. I can study 3 hours on weekdays and 6 hours on weekends. Please provide: (1) Week-by-week topic breakdown mapped to actual TCS NQT and Infosys question patterns (pattern printing, number theory, string manipulation, array rotations, basic DP), (2) Exact list of 60 must-solve problems from GeeksforGeeks and HackerRank with direct problem names, (3) Daily 3-hour schedule template (warm-up, new topic, practice, mock), (4) Aptitude-plus-coding integration tips since TCS NQT has a combined cutoff, (5) Top 15 pattern-printing and number-series questions TCS has repeated in the last 3 years, (6) Mock test cadence with which platforms to use (PrepInsta, Faceprep, iMocha), (7) A checklist of Java/C syntax gotchas Indian students commonly fail on (Scanner vs BufferedReader, integer overflow, modulo arithmetic). Format as a markdown table for the 30-day plan plus bulleted sections for the rest. Consider these constraints: I do not have paid LeetCode, my college lab uses Turbo C++ so I need portable code, and I need confidence in fundamentals over fancy algorithms.

prompts.title: Explain Time and Space Complexity For My Code
prompts.prompt: Act as a competitive programming coach with 7 years of experience teaching asymptotic analysis to GATE CSE aspirants and Indian placement candidates. I need you to analyze the time and space complexity of a piece of code I will paste. Here is the context: I am a 2nd-year CS student at a NIT who understands Big-O informally but struggles with nested loops, recursion trees, and amortized analysis. I often confuse O(n log n) with O(n^2) and get GATE complexity MCQs wrong. I will paste my code (C++, Java, or Python) right after this prompt. Please provide: (1) Line-by-line annotation of how many times each line executes in terms of input size n (and m if multiple inputs), (2) The exact time complexity in Big-O, Big-Theta, and Big-Omega with justification, (3) The exact auxiliary space complexity excluding input storage, (4) If recursion is involved, draw the recursion tree in ASCII and derive the recurrence, then solve it using Master Theorem or substitution, (5) Whether the code would pass typical GATE constraint of n <= 10^5 or LeetCode hard constraint of n <= 10^6, (6) One suggested optimization with the new complexity, (7) A similar GATE PYQ from 2015-2024 that tests the same concept. Format as markdown with a clear complexity summary table at the top. Consider these constraints: use GATE-style rigor, do not hand-wave 'approximately n', and explicitly state assumptions about hash map operations being amortized O(1). Code:

prompts.title: SQL Query Builder For DBMS Lab Assignments
prompts.prompt: Act as a DBMS lab instructor with 12 years of experience at an Indian engineering college teaching the Anna University and JNTU DBMS lab syllabus. I need you to write a correct, well-explained SQL query for a question I will paste. Here is the context: I am a 3rd-semester CS student using MySQL 8 in my college lab with tables from a standard syllabus schema (EMPLOYEE, DEPARTMENT, WORKS_ON, PROJECT from Elmasri-Navathe, or the SAILORS-BOATS-RESERVES schema from Ramakrishnan). I will paste the exact question and the relevant schema right after this prompt. Please provide: (1) The final SQL query using ANSI-standard syntax that runs on MySQL 8, (2) A step-by-step breakdown of how you decomposed the English question into relational operations (selection, projection, join, group-by, having, nested subquery), (3) An equivalent relational algebra expression since my university exam asks for it, (4) An alternative query using a different approach (e.g., correlated subquery vs JOIN, EXISTS vs IN), (5) Sample expected output on a tiny mock dataset, (6) Common mistakes students make on this type of query (e.g., using WHERE instead of HAVING, forgetting DISTINCT, division/ALL queries), (7) How this query would change if we used NOT EXISTS for the division operator. Format with fenced sql code blocks and clearly labelled sections. Consider these constraints: avoid vendor-specific features like LIMIT for ranking (use window functions or subqueries since Oracle is also used), and ensure the query handles NULLs correctly. Question and schema:

prompts.title: Operating System Scheduling Algorithm Simulator
prompts.prompt: Act as an OS professor with 15 years of experience teaching the Operating Systems course at BITS Pilani and aligned with the GATE CSE OS syllabus. I need you to solve a CPU scheduling problem step by step. Here is the context: I am a 4th-semester CS student preparing for internal exams and GATE. I will paste a process table with process IDs, arrival times, burst times, and possibly priorities right after this prompt, along with the scheduling algorithm to use (FCFS, SJF preemptive/non-preemptive, SRTF, Round Robin with quantum, Priority preemptive/non-preemptive, or Multilevel Queue). Please provide: (1) A Gantt chart drawn in ASCII with exact time markers, (2) Per-process completion time, turnaround time, waiting time, and response time in a neat markdown table, (3) Average waiting time and average turnaround time with full calculation shown, (4) Step-by-step explanation of what happens at each time unit, specifically calling out ties (which Indian textbooks and GATE resolve by arrival order then PID), (5) Whether this algorithm is preemptive or non-preemptive and whether it suffers from starvation or convoy effect for this instance, (6) How the answer would change if the quantum were halved (for RR) or if a new process arrived at t=0 with burst 1, (7) A related GATE PYQ referencing year. Format as markdown. Consider these constraints: use Galvin textbook conventions used in Indian universities, round averages to 2 decimals, and break ties by arrival time then PID. Process table:

prompts.title: Normalize a Database Schema to BCNF
prompts.prompt: Act as a DBMS tutor with 10 years of experience teaching normalization to Indian CS students following the Korth and Elmasri-Navathe textbooks used in Anna University, AKTU, and VTU syllabi. I need you to normalize a relation step by step from 1NF to BCNF. Here is the context: I am a 3rd-semester CS student who understands functional dependencies informally but struggles with identifying candidate keys, prime attributes, and applying decomposition algorithms. I will paste a relation schema R with all attributes and a set of functional dependencies F right after this prompt. Please provide: (1) All candidate keys of R derived using attribute closure, showing the closure computation for each candidate, (2) Identification of prime vs non-prime attributes, (3) Check of 2NF with explicit reasoning on partial dependencies, (4) Check of 3NF using the transitivity rule and the 3NF condition (LHS is superkey OR RHS is prime attribute), (5) Check of BCNF and identification of BCNF-violating FDs, (6) A dependency-preserving 3NF decomposition using the synthesis algorithm, (7) A BCNF decomposition using the analysis algorithm, clearly noting if dependency preservation is lost and why that is a trade-off, (8) A lossless-join verification using the chase test or the common-attribute rule. Format as markdown with clear numbered steps and boxed final answers for candidate keys and decompositions. Consider these constraints: follow GATE CSE rigor, use Armstrong axioms explicitly when computing closures, and do not skip steps even if they seem obvious. Relation and FDs:

prompts.title: Debug My C Program Segmentation Fault
prompts.prompt: Act as a C programming instructor with 12 years of experience teaching pointers, memory management, and debugging to Indian engineering students in AKTU, GTU, and Anna University colleges. I need you to find the bug in a C program that produces a segmentation fault or wrong output. Here is the context: I am a 2nd-year CS student who just learned pointers, dynamic memory, and structures. I compile with gcc on my college Linux lab machine. I will paste the full source, the input I used, the expected output, and the actual output/error right after this prompt. Please provide: (1) The exact root cause of the bug in one sentence, (2) The specific line number and the faulty expression, (3) An explanation of the underlying concept I misunderstood (dangling pointer, buffer overflow, off-by-one, uninitialized variable, double free, returning stack pointer, incorrect malloc size, missing null terminator), (4) A drawing in ASCII of the memory layout before and after the bug manifests, (5) The corrected code with only the minimal fix, clearly diffed, (6) A hardened version with defensive checks (NULL check after malloc, bounds check, free after use), (7) Three valgrind-detectable issues my code might still have and the exact valgrind command to run on my lab machine, (8) A mini-drill of 3 similar bug patterns to test my understanding. Format as markdown with fenced C code blocks. Consider these constraints: stick to C99/C11 features available in gcc 9+, do not suggest C++ features, and assume my college lab does not have online debuggers so all advice must work with printf and gdb. Code, input, expected, actual:

prompts.title: Design a URL Shortener Like Bitly
prompts.prompt: Act as a senior software engineer with 9 years of experience at Flipkart and Swiggy who regularly conducts system design interviews for Indian SDE-1 and SDE-2 candidates. I need you to walk me through a complete system design for a URL shortener like Bitly or TinyURL at the depth expected in an Amazon or Microsoft India SDE-1 interview. Here is the context: I am a final-year student at IIIT Hyderabad with strong DSA but first-time system design exposure. Assume 100 million URLs created per month, 10:1 read-to-write ratio, 5-year retention, and users in India plus SEA. Please provide: (1) Functional and non-functional requirements with explicit numeric SLAs (latency, availability), (2) Back-of-envelope capacity estimation: QPS read/write, storage per record, total storage over 5 years, bandwidth, cache size using the 80-20 rule, (3) API design with REST endpoints, request/response schemas, and HTTP status codes, (4) Database schema choice (SQL vs NoSQL) with reasoning, including the actual table/collection structure, (5) The short-key generation strategy compared across base62 encoding of auto-increment ID, MD5 hashing with collision handling, and a pre-generated key pool using Zookeeper, with a clear recommendation, (6) High-level architecture diagram described in text (client, LB, app servers, cache, DB, analytics pipeline), (7) Caching strategy with Redis (TTL, eviction, cache-aside), (8) How to handle hotspots, rate limiting, analytics, and custom aliases, (9) Three likely follow-up questions the interviewer will ask and crisp answers. Format as markdown with H2 sections matching the standard Indian interview flow. Consider these constraints: be specific with numbers, never say 'use a database', and justify every choice.

prompts.title: Dynamic Programming Pattern Recognition Trainer
prompts.prompt: Act as a DP specialist coach with 8 years of experience training Indian students for LeetCode weekly contests, Codeforces, and FAANG-India interviews. I need you to help me identify which DP pattern fits a problem and derive the recurrence from scratch. Here is the context: I am a 3rd-year CS student at a NIT who has solved basic DP (Fibonacci, climbing stairs, 0/1 knapsack) but freezes on new problems and cannot identify the state. I will paste the problem statement with constraints right after this prompt. Please provide: (1) A 5-question diagnostic checklist you mentally run through to decide if it is DP at all (optimal substructure, overlapping subproblems, choice at each step), (2) Which of the 10 canonical DP patterns it matches (linear DP, 0/1 knapsack, unbounded knapsack, LIS, LCS, matrix chain / interval DP, tree DP, bitmask DP, digit DP, DP on graphs/DAG), (3) The state definition in words and as dp[i][j] notation with exact meaning of each dimension, (4) The base cases and the recurrence relation with full derivation showing the 'choice' at each state, (5) Iterative bottom-up C++ code and top-down memoized code, both commented, (6) Space optimization (1D rolling array) if applicable, (7) Time and space complexity, (8) Two similar LeetCode problems to reinforce this exact pattern, (9) The common wrong recurrence students write for this pattern and why it fails. Format as markdown with a clear 'Pattern Identified' banner at the top. Consider these constraints: always derive the recurrence from choices, never just state it, and prefer 0-indexed arrays to match LeetCode conventions. Problem:

prompts.title: TCP IP Stack Explained With Wireshark Trace
prompts.prompt: Act as a computer networks professor with 14 years of experience teaching the Forouzan and Tanenbaum syllabus at Anna University and preparing students for GATE CSE networking section. I need you to explain what happens when a user in Bangalore types www.flipkart.com into a browser, tracing the journey through the TCP/IP stack. Here is the context: I am a 5th-semester CS student who has memorized OSI layers but does not understand how they interact in a real packet exchange. Please provide: (1) Step-by-step journey from keystroke to rendered page, naming every protocol involved (DNS, ARP, DHCP if first connection, TCP 3-way handshake, TLS 1.3 handshake, HTTP/2, HTTP/3 QUIC), (2) A layer-by-layer breakdown of the encapsulation showing application data being wrapped with HTTP headers, then TCP segment, then IP packet, then Ethernet frame, with byte counts and header field examples, (3) Exact DNS resolution flow including recursive vs iterative queries, root to TLD to authoritative, and how Jio/Airtel DNS caching affects this, (4) TCP 3-way handshake sequence diagram in ASCII with SYN/SYN-ACK/ACK and sequence numbers, (5) How congestion control (slow start, congestion avoidance, fast retransmit) kicks in on a typical Indian 4G connection with higher RTT, (6) What a Wireshark capture of this session would show filter by filter, (7) A GATE-style numerical asking how long the full page load takes given RTT 80ms, bandwidth 10 Mbps, and page size 500 KB over HTTP/1.1 vs HTTP/2. Format as markdown with ASCII diagrams. Consider these constraints: use GATE-level precision, mention header field sizes exactly, and relate to Indian ISP realities.

prompts.title: Compiler Design LR Parser Table Constructor
prompts.prompt: Act as a compiler design professor with 15 years of experience teaching the Aho-Ullman dragon book syllabus at Indian universities and setting GATE CSE compiler questions. I need you to construct a parser table for a grammar I will paste. Here is the context: I am a 6th-semester CS student in my Compiler Design course who needs to build SLR(1), CLR(1), or LALR(1) parse tables by hand for lab exams and GATE. I will paste the augmented grammar and specify which parser type right after this prompt. Please provide: (1) The augmented grammar with production numbers, (2) FIRST and FOLLOW sets for every non-terminal with the rules applied shown, (3) The canonical collection of LR(0) or LR(1) items (whichever the parser type requires), drawn state by state with goto transitions labeled, (4) A state diagram in ASCII or text showing the DFA of items, (5) The final ACTION and GOTO table in markdown tables with shift (sN), reduce (rN), accept, and blanks for error, (6) Explicit identification of any shift-reduce or reduce-reduce conflicts and whether SLR/CLR/LALR resolves them, (7) A trace of parsing one sample input string showing the stack, input, and action at each step, (8) Total number of states and whether LALR merging would reduce them vs CLR. Format as markdown with clearly titled sections and monospaced tables. Consider these constraints: follow GATE and Ullman conventions, do not skip item-set construction, and clearly mark the accept state. Grammar and parser type:

prompts.title: Page Replacement Algorithm Numerical Solver
prompts.prompt: Act as an OS tutor with 10 years of experience coaching GATE CSE aspirants on memory management numericals from Galvin and Tanenbaum. I need you to solve a page replacement problem fully. Here is the context: I am a 4th-semester CS student preparing for GATE and mid-semester exams. I will paste a reference string, the number of frames, and the algorithm (FIFO, Optimal, LRU, LFU, MFU, Second Chance/Clock, or NRU) right after this prompt. Please provide: (1) A frame-by-frame table showing the state of each frame after every reference, with HIT or MISS marked, (2) Total page faults and hit ratio as both a fraction and percentage, (3) For FIFO, whether Belady's anomaly occurs if frames are changed from k to k+1 (show the comparison), (4) For LRU, the stack or counter simulation method, (5) For Optimal, show the 'farthest future use' computation at each replacement, (6) Step-by-step narration of which page is evicted and why, (7) Comparison table showing faults for this reference string across FIFO, LRU, and Optimal, (8) A GATE PYQ from 2010-2023 that tests this exact pattern with the year mentioned, (9) Common traps: tie-breaking in LRU, counting initial loads as faults (which Indian textbooks do). Format as markdown with monospaced frame tables. Consider these constraints: count cold misses as page faults per Galvin convention, break LRU ties by the least recently added, and show all calculation steps for full-marks answer writing in GATE-style exams. Reference string and parameters:

prompts.title: Graph Algorithms From Adjacency To Shortest Path
prompts.prompt: Act as a graph algorithms specialist with 8 years of experience coaching Indian students for ICPC regionals and Amazon/Google India interviews. I need you to solve a graph problem completely with code and trace. Here is the context: I am a 3rd-year CS student at BITS Pilani who understands BFS and DFS but gets lost on Dijkstra, Bellman-Ford, Floyd-Warshall, MST, topological sort, and SCC. I will paste the problem and graph (edge list or adjacency matrix) right after this prompt. Please provide: (1) Identification of which graph algorithm fits and why (directed vs undirected, weighted vs unweighted, negative edges allowed, single-source vs all-pairs, cycle detection needed), (2) Clean C++ implementation using adjacency list with vector<pair<int,int>>, commented, (3) A full dry run on the input graph showing the priority queue / distance array / visited set at every iteration, drawn in a markdown table, (4) Time and space complexity with justification (why Dijkstra is O((V+E) log V) with a binary heap), (5) Why a naive approach like repeated BFS fails on weighted graphs, (6) Edge cases (disconnected components, self-loops, parallel edges, negative cycles), (7) One modification of the problem (e.g., second-shortest path, k-shortest paths, shortest path with exactly k edges) and how to adapt the solution, (8) A similar LeetCode problem to practice. Format as markdown with a dry-run table. Consider these constraints: never use Python-only tricks, handle 1-indexed vs 0-indexed conversion explicitly, and test for integer overflow when summing edge weights. Problem and graph:

prompts.title: OOPs Concepts For Java Interview
prompts.prompt: Act as a Java trainer with 10 years of experience preparing Indian freshers for Infosys Mysore training, TCS digital, and Capgemini technical interviews. I need you to give me a complete, interview-ready breakdown of OOPs concepts with Java examples. Here is the context: I am a final-year CS student whose interviews always start with 'Explain the four pillars of OOP' and then drill into Java specifics like overriding vs overloading, abstract class vs interface (Java 8+), and why Java does not support multiple inheritance. Please provide: (1) The four pillars (encapsulation, inheritance, polymorphism, abstraction) each defined in one sentence plus a runnable Java 17 example that is non-trivial (not the usual Animal->Dog), ideally using a Bank or Employee domain, (2) Overriding vs overloading with a tricky code snippet asking what gets printed and why, covering method resolution order, (3) Abstract class vs interface including default and static methods added in Java 8 and private methods in Java 9, with a decision rubric on when to use which, (4) Why Java does not support multiple inheritance of classes, how the diamond problem is resolved with interfaces, (5) 'is-a' vs 'has-a' relationship with code, (6) Access modifiers table and their visibility scope, (7) 10 real interview questions asked at TCS, Infosys, and Wipro in the last 2 years with 3-line model answers each, (8) 3 tricky output-prediction snippets for static binding, dynamic dispatch, and constructor call order. Format as markdown with fenced java code blocks. Consider these constraints: use Java 17 since most Indian service companies have upgraded, avoid Scala-esque syntax, and every concept must be shown in code not just defined.

prompts.title: Deadlock Detection And Avoidance Banker Algorithm
prompts.prompt: Act as an OS instructor with 12 years of experience teaching concurrency and deadlocks from the Galvin textbook to Indian CS undergraduates. I need you to solve a deadlock numerical completely. Here is the context: I am a 4th-semester CS student at an AKTU college preparing for my OS end-semester and GATE. I will paste the number of processes, number of resource types, Allocation matrix, Max matrix, and Available vector right after this prompt. Please provide: (1) Computation of the Need matrix from Max - Allocation, shown cell by cell, (2) Application of the Banker's safety algorithm showing Work and Finish at every iteration, with the safe sequence at the end or a clear declaration of unsafe state, (3) If the question asks 'can request R from process P be granted', simulate the hypothetical grant and re-run the safety check, (4) State whether the system is in deadlock using resource-allocation graph reasoning (cycle necessary but not sufficient when multiple instances exist), (5) ASCII resource-allocation graph with request edges and assignment edges, (6) Comparison of this approach to deadlock prevention (Coffman conditions violation) and deadlock ignorance (ostrich), (7) Two GATE PYQs on Banker's algorithm with year references and quick answers, (8) The time complexity of Banker's algorithm in terms of number of processes n and resource types m. Format as markdown with clean matrix tables. Consider these constraints: use GATE rigor, break ties in process selection by lowest PID, and clearly label Work vector at each step. Problem data:

prompts.title: Design WhatsApp Chat Backend System
prompts.prompt: Act as a principal engineer with 12 years of experience at Meta and PhonePe who interviews candidates for SDE-2 and SDE-3 roles across Bangalore and Hyderabad. I need you to design a WhatsApp-like chat backend at interview depth. Here is the context: I am a final-year M.Tech student at IIT Madras preparing for Google, Meta, and Uber India interviews. Assume 500 million users, 50 billion messages per day, end-to-end encryption, last-seen, typing indicators, and offline message delivery. Please provide: (1) Functional and non-functional requirements with explicit SLAs for message delivery latency (p50, p99), (2) Back-of-envelope estimation: QPS, storage per day/year, bandwidth, active connections, (3) Why long-polling/HTTP is wrong and why persistent connections via WebSocket or XMPP are chosen, with connection-per-user math, (4) High-level architecture: gateway, connection service, message service, presence service, media service, notification service for offline push via FCM/APNs, (5) Data model for messages, conversations, and receipts with choice of database (Cassandra/HBase for messages, Redis for presence, object store for media) with justification, (6) Message delivery flow for 1-to-1, group (with fan-out-on-write vs fan-out-on-read trade-off for Indian 256-member groups), and offline case, (7) End-to-end encryption at a high level (Signal protocol double ratchet), (8) How to shard by user ID or conversation ID and why, (9) Three follow-up questions an interviewer will ask (read receipts, message ordering, multi-device sync) with crisp answers. Format as markdown with H2 sections. Consider these constraints: use real numbers, justify every tech choice, and mention the CAP trade-offs explicitly.

prompts.title: Regex And Finite Automata Converter
prompts.prompt: Act as a Theory of Computation tutor with 10 years of experience teaching the Ullman-Hopcroft TOC syllabus to GATE CSE aspirants and Indian CS undergraduates. I need you to convert between regular expressions, NFA, DFA, and minimized DFA for a problem I will paste. Here is the context: I am a 5th-semester CS student who struggles with the subset construction and DFA minimization for my Formal Languages and Automata Theory course. I will paste either a regex or an automaton right after this prompt and specify the direction of conversion. Please provide: (1) If converting regex to NFA, apply Thompson's construction step by step showing each sub-NFA, (2) If converting NFA to DFA, apply the subset construction algorithm explicitly listing epsilon-closures, move sets, and the resulting DFA state table, (3) If minimizing a DFA, use the equivalence partitioning algorithm (Hopcroft or table-filling) with every partition iteration shown, (4) Final DFA drawn in ASCII with states as circles/double circles for accept, and labeled transitions, (5) State count comparison: original NFA states, unminimized DFA states, minimized DFA states, (6) 3 sample strings and whether they are accepted, traced through the minimized DFA, (7) The equivalent regex if minimization resulted in a clearly simpler machine, (8) A similar GATE PYQ with year reference. Format as markdown with monospaced state tables and ASCII automata. Consider these constraints: use GATE conventions, label the start state q0, and handle the dead state explicitly when the DFA is not total. Input and conversion direction:

prompts.title: HR And Behavioral Round For CS Freshers
prompts.prompt: Act as an HR manager with 11 years of experience hiring CS freshers at TCS, Infosys, Wipro, Accenture, and Capgemini in India. I need you to prepare me for the HR round of campus placements. Here is the context: I am a final-year CS student from a Tier-2 engineering college in Pune with a CGPA of 7.8, one MERN-stack internship, and one hackathon win. I am nervous about HR questions, especially 'Tell me about yourself', 'Why this company', 'Weaknesses', 'Relocation anywhere in India', 'Bond of 2 years', and 'Salary expectations for fresher'. Please provide: (1) A 90-second 'Tell me about yourself' script tailored to my profile that front-loads CS skills, internship, and one personal story, (2) Company-specific 'Why TCS/Infosys/Wipro' answers that cite real initiatives (TCS iON, Infosys Springboard, Wipro Turbo) and avoid generic praise, (3) STAR-format answers for 5 behavioral questions: teamwork conflict, failure, leadership, tight deadline, learning a new tech, (4) How to honestly discuss a 7.8 CGPA without being defensive, (5) Handling service bond questions without sounding mercenary, (6) Red-flag phrases that get Indian freshers rejected (e.g., 'I will join only Bangalore', 'I want to leave in 2 years for higher studies'), (7) 10 rapid-fire HR questions with crisp 3-line answers, (8) Questions I should ask the interviewer at the end to seem engaged. Format as markdown with headers per question. Consider these constraints: Indian service-company culture values loyalty and humility, avoid aggressive Western-style confidence, and do not fabricate experiences.

prompts.title: REST API Design For College Mini Project
prompts.prompt: Act as a full-stack engineer with 7 years of experience at Razorpay building REST APIs and mentoring Indian CS final-year students on their mini-projects. I need you to design a complete REST API for a project idea I will describe. Here is the context: I am a 7th-semester CS student at an Anna University college building my mini project which must be submitted with documentation including API design. I will paste my project idea (e.g., college attendance system, library management, food-ordering clone, blood-donation portal) right after this prompt. Please provide: (1) List of entities and their attributes with primary keys, (2) Complete REST endpoint design following RFC 7231: resource-oriented URLs, correct HTTP verbs (GET, POST, PUT, PATCH, DELETE), plural nouns, nesting only 1 level deep, (3) Request and response JSON schemas for each endpoint with sample payloads using Indian context (names like Arjun, cities like Chennai, INR amounts), (4) HTTP status codes for success, client errors, and server errors with when to use 200 vs 201 vs 204, 400 vs 401 vs 403 vs 404 vs 409 vs 422, (5) Authentication strategy (JWT with refresh tokens) and how to protect routes, (6) Pagination, filtering, and sorting query parameters following a consistent convention, (7) Error response envelope format, (8) OpenAPI 3.0 snippet for two key endpoints, (9) A quick note on which endpoints would need rate limiting and idempotency keys. Format as markdown with fenced json code blocks. Consider these constraints: the project must be demoable on localhost with Node.js/Express or Spring Boot, avoid over-engineering for a mini project, and design should be submittable as a 3-page API document.

prompts.title: Greedy Vs Dynamic Programming Decision Guide
prompts.prompt: Act as an algorithms lecturer with 9 years of experience teaching from CLRS to Indian CS students and writing GATE CSE algorithms questions. I need you to decide whether a problem should be solved greedily or with DP and justify it rigorously. Here is the context: I am a 3rd-year CS student who picks greedy because it is easier and then gets wrong answers on LeetCode. I will paste the problem statement right after this prompt. Please provide: (1) A checklist you run to decide greedy vs DP (greedy-choice property, optimal substructure, exchange argument, overlapping subproblems), (2) An attempt at a greedy strategy, stated precisely (e.g., 'always pick the smallest remaining'), (3) A proof attempt of the greedy via exchange argument, AND either a counterexample that disproves it OR a clean proof that validates it, (4) If greedy fails, the DP formulation with state, recurrence, base case, and complexity, (5) If greedy works, clean code with proof-inspired comments, (6) Side-by-side table: greedy complexity vs DP complexity for this problem, (7) A classic GATE or LeetCode problem that 'looks greedy but is DP' (coin change, activity selection vs weighted interval scheduling, etc.) for contrast, (8) One rule of thumb to remember for this problem class. Format as markdown with labeled H2 sections. Consider these constraints: always test greedy with at least one counterexample attempt before accepting it, and never claim a proof without either an exchange argument or an induction sketch. Problem:

prompts.title: B Plus Tree And Indexing Numerical
prompts.prompt: Act as a DBMS professor with 13 years of experience teaching file organization and indexing from Korth and Elmasri-Navathe at Indian universities and setting GATE CSE disk-based numericals. I need you to solve a B+ tree indexing numerical completely. Here is the context: I am a 5th-semester CS student who finds disk-block numericals confusing, especially calculating order, fan-out, number of levels, and insertion/deletion. I will paste the problem parameters (block size, key size, pointer size, record size, number of records, dense vs sparse) right after this prompt. Please provide: (1) Computation of B+ tree order for internal nodes using (block_size - pointer) / (key + pointer) + 1, shown step by step, (2) Computation of leaf-node order using (block_size - next_pointer) / (key + record_pointer), (3) Number of levels of the tree as ceiling of log base (order/2) of total leaf entries, (4) Total number of disk accesses to find a record given a key, (5) Step-by-step insertion of 5 keys into an empty B+ tree of the derived order showing splits and propagations, drawn in ASCII, (6) Step-by-step deletion of 2 keys showing borrow and merge cases, (7) Primary vs secondary index comparison and which this problem describes, (8) Difference between B tree and B+ tree and why B+ tree is chosen for databases (range queries over linked leaves), (9) A GATE PYQ from the last 10 years on B+ tree order calculation with year reference. Format as markdown with ASCII tree diagrams. Consider these constraints: use GATE conventions for order definition (some books differ), show ceiling/floor explicitly, and assume unclustered secondary index unless stated otherwise. Parameters:

prompts.title: Git And GitHub For College Project Submission
prompts.prompt: Act as a DevOps engineer with 6 years of experience at a Bangalore startup who mentors Indian CS students on open-source contributions and final-year project workflows. I need you to teach me a complete Git workflow for my team project. Here is the context: I am a 3rd-year CS student working with 3 teammates on a final-year project repo hosted on GitHub. None of us have used Git beyond 'git add .' and 'git commit -m' and we keep overwriting each other's code. Please provide: (1) A step-by-step setup from scratch: creating the repo, adding collaborators, setting up SSH keys on Windows and Ubuntu lab PCs, (2) A branching strategy appropriate for a 4-person student team (main + feature branches, no need for full GitFlow), (3) The exact 10 commands we will use daily (git pull, fetch, checkout -b, add -p, commit, push, log, status, diff, stash) with one-line explanations, (4) How to write good commit messages with the conventional commits format (feat:, fix:, docs:), (5) Step-by-step pull request workflow with code review checklist, (6) How to resolve a merge conflict when two teammates edit the same file, with exact commands and VS Code workflow, (7) How to undo common mistakes: unstage a file, revert a commit, drop uncommitted changes, recover a deleted branch, (8) A .gitignore template for a MERN or Spring Boot project, (9) How to show the repo to external examiners during viva with a clean commit history and README. Format as markdown with fenced bash code blocks. Consider these constraints: commands must work on Windows Git Bash since most Indian college labs run Windows, avoid GUI-only suggestions, and explain every flag.

prompts.title: Semaphore And Mutex Concurrency Problem
prompts.prompt: Act as an OS instructor with 12 years of experience teaching process synchronization from Galvin and Silberschatz to Indian CS undergraduates and GATE aspirants. I need you to solve a classical synchronization problem using semaphores or mutexes. Here is the context: I am a 4th-semester CS student who can recite Peterson's algorithm but cannot write pseudocode for producer-consumer, reader-writer, dining philosophers, sleeping barber, or cigarette smokers. I will specify the problem and any variant (bounded buffer size, writer preference vs reader preference, 5 vs n philosophers, starvation-free requirement) right after this prompt. Please provide: (1) A clear statement of the shared variables and semaphores with initial values, (2) Complete pseudocode for every process/thread type with wait() and signal() operations, indented clearly, (3) A walkthrough showing how the solution avoids race conditions, deadlock, and starvation, (4) Identification of which of the 4 Coffman conditions for deadlock is broken and how, (5) An incorrect 'first-attempt' version that students commonly write and the specific interleaving that breaks it, (6) How the solution changes if we use monitors with condition variables instead of semaphores, (7) Variant discussion: what if we add a second resource, or require FIFO fairness, (8) A GATE PYQ on semaphores from the last 15 years with year and quick answer, (9) Real-world mapping (e.g., producer-consumer is Kafka, reader-writer is a cache). Format as markdown with fenced pseudocode blocks. Consider these constraints: use P() / V() or wait() / signal() notation consistently, never mix, and clearly mark the critical section. Problem:

prompts.title: HLD For E-Commerce Order Service
prompts.prompt: Act as a staff engineer with 11 years of experience at Flipkart and Myntra who has designed order-processing systems handling Big Billion Days traffic. I need you to produce a high-level design for an e-commerce order service. Here is the context: I am a final-year M.Tech student preparing for SDE-2 interviews at Flipkart, Amazon, and Zomato. Assume 10 million orders per day on normal days and 10x on sale days, multi-city warehouses across India, COD and prepaid, cart-to-order conversion, inventory reservation, and payment integration with Razorpay/UPI. Please provide: (1) Functional and non-functional requirements with SLAs, (2) Capacity estimation for peak sale-day QPS, storage, and DB connections, (3) Service decomposition: cart service, order service, inventory service, payment service, notification service, with boundaries and ownership, (4) Data model for orders (with state machine: CREATED, PAYMENT_PENDING, PAID, PACKED, SHIPPED, DELIVERED, CANCELLED, REFUNDED) and inventory, (5) Synchronous vs asynchronous flows, specifically why payment is sync and shipping notification is async via Kafka, (6) Handling inventory oversell during flash sales: optimistic vs pessimistic locking, Redis-based atomic counters, or distributed locks, (7) Idempotency of order creation API using client-generated idempotency keys, (8) Saga pattern vs 2PC for the order creation transaction spanning multiple services, (9) Failure modes: payment succeeds but order fails, inventory reserved but payment times out, and how to reconcile, (10) Three likely follow-up questions an interviewer will ask. Format as markdown with H2 sections mirroring the Flipkart interview rubric. Consider these constraints: use real numbers, mention CAP trade-offs, and justify every tech choice.

prompts.title: Python Code Review For Data Structures Assignment
prompts.prompt: Act as a senior Python developer with 8 years of experience and a part-time CS lab assistant at an Indian engineering college. I need you to review my Python code for a data structures assignment and suggest improvements. Here is the context: I am a 2nd-year CS student who writes Python like I write C: for loops with index variables, no list comprehensions, no use of collections.deque, heapq, or defaultdict. I will paste my code and the assignment problem right after this prompt. Please provide: (1) Correctness verification: does the code actually solve the problem, with specific test cases you traced, (2) Time and space complexity of my current code, (3) A Pythonic refactor using idiomatic constructs (list/dict/set comprehensions, enumerate, zip, collections module, heapq, bisect) with side-by-side before/after for each change, (4) Specific PEP-8 style fixes (naming, spacing, line length), (5) Bugs or edge cases I missed (empty input, single element, duplicates, negative numbers), (6) Whether my chosen data structure is wrong (e.g., list used where set is needed for O(1) lookup), (7) A second refactor that prioritizes readability over cleverness, (8) Tips on using Python's built-in data structures that LeetCode and placements reward (heapq for priority queue, deque for O(1) popleft, Counter for frequency, OrderedDict for LRU). Format as markdown with fenced python code blocks and diff-style highlights. Consider these constraints: stick to Python 3.8+ since that is what most Indian college labs have, do not recommend numpy for a DS assignment, and keep refactors understandable to a 2nd-year student. Assignment and code:

prompts.title: Subnetting And VLSM Numerical For Networks
prompts.prompt: Act as a networking instructor with 10 years of experience teaching IP addressing and subnetting from Forouzan and Kurose-Ross at Indian universities and to CCNA aspirants. I need you to solve a subnetting or VLSM problem step by step. Here is the context: I am a 5th-semester CS student who memorizes subnet masks but cannot solve VLSM numericals for assignments and GATE. I will paste the problem (typically: given an IP block like 192.168.10.0/24, divide it among subnets with X hosts each) right after this prompt. Please provide: (1) Conversion of the given block into binary and decimal with the exact number of host bits and network bits, (2) For each required subnet, calculation of minimum host bits needed using ceil(log2(hosts + 2)), (3) VLSM allocation in descending order of size, showing each subnet's network address, broadcast address, usable host range, and subnet mask in both dotted decimal and CIDR notation, (4) A summary table with all subnets, (5) Verification that subnets do not overlap and total usage fits within the original block, (6) Calculation of wasted addresses, (7) Handling of special addresses (network, broadcast, gateway convention of first usable), (8) How classless routing differs from classful and why VLSM requires CIDR, (9) A GATE PYQ on subnetting from 2015-2023 with year reference. Format as markdown with a monospaced subnet table and binary breakdowns. Consider these constraints: use Forouzan and Indian textbook conventions (always reserve 2 addresses per subnet), show binary carefully, and do not use shortcut formulas without derivation. Problem:

prompts.title: Generate Test Cases For My LeetCode Solution
prompts.prompt: Act as a competitive programming judge with 7 years of experience writing test data for CodeChef Long Challenge and Indian college coding contests. I need you to generate a comprehensive test suite for a DSA problem and my solution. Here is the context: I am a final-year CS student who passes LeetCode sample tests but fails on hidden cases during contests, losing easy ratings. I will paste the problem statement, my code, and the constraint bounds right after this prompt. Please provide: (1) 5 sanity tests including the given samples with expected output, (2) 5 edge cases: minimum input (n=0 or 1), maximum input stress (n at upper bound), all same values, all distinct sorted, all distinct reverse sorted, (3) 5 tricky cases: negative numbers if allowed, zeros, duplicates, overflow-triggering inputs (sum of n * max_value beyond int range), boundary of the mathematical constraint, (4) 3 worst-case performance tests where a naive O(n^2) would TLE but the intended O(n log n) would pass, with the exact expected runtime on LeetCode's C++ judge, (5) For each test, a one-line explanation of what it is designed to catch, (6) Three common wrong-answer patterns my solution might exhibit (off-by-one in binary search, integer overflow, incorrect comparator returning negative from subtraction), (7) A random test generator in Python (10 lines) I can paste into my IDE to run against a brute force, (8) A brute-force reference solution for small n to compare against. Format as markdown with a test table and fenced code. Consider these constraints: tests must be in the problem's exact input format, cover GATE/LeetCode convention of 1-indexed or 0-indexed as specified, and include both expected stdout and notes. Problem and code:

prompts.title: CAP Theorem And Distributed Systems Explainer
prompts.prompt: Act as a distributed systems lecturer with 12 years of experience teaching from Kleppmann's DDIA and training engineers at Indian product companies like Zerodha and PhonePe. I need you to explain the CAP theorem rigorously with Indian real-world examples. Here is the context: I am a final-year CS student who can recite 'CAP is Consistency, Availability, Partition tolerance, pick 2' but gets confused in interviews when asked 'what kind of consistency' or 'why is partition tolerance not optional'. Please provide: (1) A precise definition of each C, A, and P with formal correctness, clearly stating that C in CAP is linearizability not ACID-consistency, (2) Why 'pick 2' is misleading and why during normal operation you get both C and A, and only during a partition must you choose between C and A (Abadi's PACELC extension), (3) A real scenario with two Indian data centers (Mumbai and Bangalore) losing connectivity and walking through how a CP system like HBase vs an AP system like Cassandra behaves, (4) Mapping 10 real systems into CP or AP (MongoDB, Cassandra, DynamoDB, HBase, Zookeeper, etcd, Redis, MySQL with replication, Kafka, ElasticSearch), (5) The consistency spectrum: linearizable, sequential, causal, read-your-writes, eventual, with Indian UPI examples (why UPI needs strong, why Instagram likes can be eventual), (6) Quorum consistency math (R + W > N) with a worked example at N=3, (7) 5 interview-level questions with answers, (8) Why PACELC is now more relevant than CAP for modern databases. Format as markdown with tables and H2 sections. Consider these constraints: be rigorous and name specific papers (Gilbert-Lynch 2002), avoid hand-waving, and relate every concept to Indian product-engineering context.

prompts.title: Linked List Operations With Drawings
prompts.prompt: Act as a DSA tutor with 8 years of experience teaching first-year CS students at Indian engineering colleges. I need you to teach me linked list operations with clear ASCII drawings and clean code. Here is the context: I am a 2nd-semester CS student who just started DSA and keeps getting confused by pointer manipulation in singly and doubly linked lists. My exam has questions on insertion, deletion, reversal, finding the middle, detecting a cycle, and merging two sorted lists. Please provide: (1) A clear struct/class definition for singly and doubly linked list nodes in C++ and Java, (2) Step-by-step ASCII drawings for insertion at head, at tail, at arbitrary position, and at sorted position, showing the before and after state with the exact pointer updates numbered 1,2,3, (3) Deletion at head, tail, and arbitrary position with the same ASCII discipline, (4) Iterative reversal of a singly linked list with the classic 'prev, curr, next' three-pointer approach, drawn step by step for a 4-node list, (5) Recursive reversal with the base case and recursion tree, (6) Floyd's tortoise-and-hare cycle detection with a proof sketch of why slow and fast meet, (7) Merge two sorted linked lists with a dummy-head technique, (8) Common bugs: losing the head pointer, dereferencing NULL, forgetting to free deleted nodes in C++, (9) 5 practice problems from LeetCode with links by problem number (e.g., LC 206, LC 141, LC 21, LC 876, LC 92). Format as markdown with lots of ASCII diagrams. Consider these constraints: use code that works on standard Indian college C++ compilers, always free deleted nodes in C/C++, and keep diagrams simple enough to reproduce on exam paper.

prompts.title: Write Resume Bullet Points For CS Internship
prompts.prompt: Act as a career coach with 9 years of experience reviewing resumes for Indian CS students applying to internships at Flipkart, Swiggy, Zomato, Razorpay, Microsoft IDC, and Google India. I need you to rewrite my project and internship bullet points into high-impact, ATS-friendly resume entries. Here is the context: I am a pre-final-year CS student at a Tier-2 college applying for summer internships on LinkedIn and through referrals. My current bullets are vague like 'Developed a website using React' or 'Worked on a machine learning project'. I will paste my current bullets, the technology used, and what I actually did right after this prompt. Please provide: (1) The XYZ formula rewrite for each bullet: Accomplished X as measured by Y by doing Z, quantified with numbers wherever possible (latency, users, data size, accuracy), (2) Action verbs that specifically impress Indian tech recruiters (Architected, Optimized, Scaled, Automated, Reduced, Shipped), (3) Two versions per bullet: one for product companies (Flipkart, Razorpay) emphasizing scale and impact, one for service companies (TCS, Accenture) emphasizing teamwork and SDLC, (4) ATS keyword density check: for each bullet, which JD keywords from a typical SDE intern posting are present, (5) Removal of weak phrases (helped, worked on, assisted, responsible for) and what to replace them with, (6) A suggested 1-page resume structure for a CS junior (Education, Skills, Experience, Projects, Achievements) with word counts per section, (7) 5 metrics I should try to collect from my projects even retroactively (file size, response time, lines of code, users, DB rows), (8) 3 bullets for a MERN project and 3 for a DSA GitHub repo as gold-standard examples. Format as markdown with before/after tables. Consider these constraints: keep it truthful, do not invent metrics, and use Indian-school-relevant projects.

prompts.title: Docker For Final Year Project Deployment
prompts.prompt: Act as a DevOps engineer with 7 years of experience at an Indian SaaS startup who mentors students on deploying their final-year projects. I need you to dockerize my full-stack final-year project so I can demo it on any examiner's laptop. Here is the context: I am a final-year CS student with a MERN-stack project (React frontend, Node/Express backend, MongoDB) that currently runs only on my machine because of version and env issues. I will paste my project structure and package.json snippets right after this prompt. Please provide: (1) A production-grade multi-stage Dockerfile for the React frontend with an Nginx serving stage, (2) A Dockerfile for the Node/Express backend using node:18-alpine with a non-root user and proper layering for cache efficiency, (3) A docker-compose.yml that runs frontend, backend, and MongoDB with a shared network and named volumes for persistent data, (4) Environment variable management using a .env file and how to safely .gitignore it, (5) Healthcheck directives for each service, (6) Exact commands to build, run, stop, and inspect logs, (7) How to expose the backend only to the frontend container and not to the host, (8) A 5-minute 'demo script' I can run in front of examiners: docker compose up, open localhost, show working features, (9) Troubleshooting: port-already-in-use, MongoDB connection refused, CORS errors between containers, (10) How to push the images to Docker Hub so the examiner can pull them without my laptop. Format as markdown with fenced dockerfile, yaml, and bash blocks. Consider these constraints: must work on Windows Docker Desktop and Linux, image sizes should be under 200 MB for frontend, and the whole thing must come up with a single 'docker compose up'.

prompts.title: Hashing Collision Resolution GATE Numerical
prompts.prompt: Act as a DSA and GATE CSE coach with 11 years of experience teaching hash tables from CLRS to Indian students. I need you to solve a hashing numerical completely. Here is the context: I am a 3rd-year CS student preparing for GATE and placement exams. I will paste the hash table size, hash function, collision resolution method (separate chaining, linear probing, quadratic probing, or double hashing), and the sequence of keys to insert right after this prompt. Please provide: (1) The table drawn as a row of slots, updated after every insertion, (2) For each key, the computed hash value with the modulo arithmetic shown, (3) For each collision, the probe sequence with h(k), h(k)+1, h(k)+4, ... until a free slot is found, shown explicitly, (4) For double hashing, both hash functions evaluated at every step, (5) Number of comparisons or probes per insertion, with total and average, (6) Load factor after all insertions and whether rehashing would be triggered at a typical threshold of 0.75, (7) How lookup for one successful key and one unsuccessful key would proceed, (8) Primary clustering vs secondary clustering discussion and which this method suffers from, (9) A GATE PYQ on hashing from the last 10 years with year reference and a quick solution, (10) Comparison of average-case O(1) vs worst-case O(n) with when each occurs. Format as markdown with a monospaced slot table updated at each step. Consider these constraints: use GATE conventions, start slot indexing at 0, and clearly mark deleted slots with a tombstone for open addressing deletions if the problem asks. Parameters and key sequence:

prompts.title: Intermediate Code Generation For Expressions
prompts.prompt: Act as a compiler design professor with 14 years of experience teaching the Aho-Ullman dragon book at Indian universities and setting GATE CSE compiler questions. I need you to generate three-address code, quadruples, triples, and indirect triples for an expression or program fragment. Here is the context: I am a 6th-semester CS student in Compiler Design preparing for end semester and GATE. I will paste the source expression or statement (for example, a = b * c + d * e, or an if-else, or a while loop) right after this prompt. Please provide: (1) The syntax tree drawn in ASCII, (2) The DAG with common subexpressions merged and numbered, (3) Three-address code using temporary variables t1, t2, t3 with one operator per instruction, (4) Quadruples representation in a markdown table with op, arg1, arg2, result columns, (5) Triples representation with op, arg1, arg2 columns and references by index, (6) Indirect triples with a pointer list plus the triples list, (7) For control flow (if/while), the backpatching approach with true-list, false-list, and nextlist, showing how labels get resolved, (8) Code comparison: the number of instructions with and without DAG optimization, (9) The advantage of each representation (quadruples for optimization, triples for memory, indirect for reordering), (10) A GATE PYQ on three-address code or quadruples with year reference. Format as markdown with monospaced tables. Consider these constraints: follow dragon book conventions used in Indian CS curricula, use t1, t2, ... as temporary names, and number instructions starting from 100 for GATE-style answers. Expression or code:

prompts.title: Mock Technical Interview DSA Round
prompts.prompt: Act as a Bar Raiser interviewer at Amazon Bangalore with 9 years of experience conducting SDE-1 coding rounds for Indian campus hires. I need you to conduct a 45-minute mock DSA interview on me via text. Here is the context: I am a final-year student at a Tier-2 engineering college with 200+ LeetCode problems solved, targeting SDE roles at Amazon, Microsoft, Flipkart. I want an honest simulation, not hand-holding. Please run the mock in this exact flow: (1) Start with a 2-minute warm-up asking me to introduce myself and then tell me the problem (pick one from: Kth largest in stream, LRU cache, Number of islands, Word ladder, or Course schedule II - pick whichever I have not told you I already did), (2) Present the problem statement with one sample input and one constraint (1 <= n <= 10^5), (3) Wait for me to ask clarifying questions and give realistic clarifications in character, (4) Wait for me to propose a brute-force approach before optimizing, and push back if I jump to optimal without discussing brute, (5) Ask me to code the solution in C++ or Java and critique my code for bugs, naming, and edge cases as a real interviewer would, (6) Ask me to trace through a tricky test case, (7) Ask follow-up questions scaling the problem (what if data does not fit in memory, what if streaming), (8) End with 2 behavioral questions: 'Tell me about a time you missed a deadline' and 'Why Amazon', (9) Give me detailed feedback scored on: problem clarification, approach communication, code quality, debugging, complexity analysis, behavioral. Rate each on 1-5 and give a hire/no-hire verdict. Format as a multi-turn dialogue that pauses and waits for my response at each step. Consider these constraints: stay in character as a strict but fair interviewer, do not give hints unless I ask, and be honest in the verdict.

prompts.title: Explain Transaction Isolation Levels
prompts.prompt: Act as a DBMS engineer with 10 years of experience at an Indian fintech company (Razorpay or CRED) who has debugged production concurrency issues in PostgreSQL and MySQL. I need you to explain SQL transaction isolation levels with concrete examples that a CS student can use in interviews and university exams. Here is the context: I am a 5th-semester CS student who has read the definitions of Read Uncommitted, Read Committed, Repeatable Read, and Serializable but cannot clearly explain dirty reads, non-repeatable reads, phantom reads, or lost updates. Please provide: (1) The ACID properties in one-line definitions with atomicity vs consistency clearly distinguished (both get confused by students), (2) A table mapping each isolation level to which anomalies it prevents and allows, (3) For each of the 4 anomalies, a concrete 2-transaction timeline with T1 and T2 interleaving, using a fintech example (T1 reads balance to display, T2 debits, T1 transfers based on stale read), showing timestamps and SQL statements, (4) How PostgreSQL's snapshot-isolation based Repeatable Read differs from MySQL InnoDB's gap-locking based Repeatable Read and why this matters for phantom reads, (5) The Serializable level implementations: two-phase locking vs serializable snapshot isolation (SSI), (6) When to use each level in practice, with the default in PostgreSQL, MySQL, and Oracle noted, (7) Deadlock scenario in Repeatable Read and how the DB resolves it, (8) 3 university exam questions and 3 interview questions on isolation levels with sharp answers, (9) A GATE PYQ from the last 10 years. Format as markdown with timeline tables. Consider these constraints: use real SQL snippets, Indian fintech examples with INR amounts, and be precise about vendor differences.

prompts.title: Analyze Code Snippet Output Prediction
prompts.prompt: Act as a placement trainer with 9 years of experience preparing Indian CS students for TCS NQT, Infosys DSE, and Capgemini pseudocode rounds where output-prediction questions are common. I need you to analyze a code snippet and predict its exact output, step by step. Here is the context: I am a final-year CS student who often gets output questions wrong because of operator precedence, integer vs floating division, pass-by-value vs pass-by-reference, static vs instance variables, and tricky loop conditions. I will paste the snippet (C, C++, Java, or Python) right after this prompt. Please provide: (1) Identification of the language and any language-specific gotchas to watch for, (2) Line-by-line execution trace with the state of every variable after each statement, shown in a markdown table, (3) The exact printed output character by character including newlines, (4) Identification of the 'trick' in the question: typical tricks are ++i vs i++, short-circuit evaluation, static variable retention, integer overflow, array index out of bound, string immutability, default argument evaluation in Python, (5) What the output would be if we changed one specific line (commonly asked follow-up), (6) The concept being tested that students should remember for future questions, (7) A similar snippet from TCS NQT 2023 or Infosys 2024 with answer, (8) A common wrong answer students pick and the misconception behind it. Format as markdown with a clear variable-state table and the final output in a fenced block. Consider these constraints: trace every variable do not skip steps, be precise about spaces and newlines in output, and explicitly state compiler assumptions (e.g., 32-bit int, left-to-right evaluation where defined). Code snippet:

prompts.title: Minimum Spanning Tree Kruskal And Prim Numerical
prompts.prompt: Act as a DSA professor with 10 years of experience teaching graph algorithms to GATE CSE aspirants and Indian CS undergraduates. I need you to solve an MST problem using both Kruskal's and Prim's algorithms. Here is the context: I am a 4th-semester CS student who understands the idea of MST but struggles with tie-breaking, union-find operations, and manually tracing Prim's with a priority queue. I will paste the graph (vertices and weighted edges, undirected) right after this prompt. Please provide: (1) The graph drawn in ASCII or described as an adjacency list, (2) Kruskal's algorithm trace: sorted edge list with weights, then for each edge in order, show the union-find parent array state, whether the edge is added or rejected (cycle formed), and the running MST weight, (3) Final MST from Kruskal with total weight and the set of selected edges, (4) Prim's algorithm trace starting from vertex 0 (or specified source): at each step show the priority queue / key array, the vertex extracted, and which edges get relaxed, (5) Final MST from Prim with total weight, (6) Verification that both algorithms produce the same total weight (even if edges differ due to ties), (7) Time complexity with union-find path compression and union by rank for Kruskal O(E log E), and with binary heap for Prim O((V+E) log V), (8) Discussion: when is Prim better (dense graphs), when is Kruskal better (sparse graphs), (9) A GATE PYQ on MST from 2010-2023 with year reference, (10) Whether this MST is unique and how to check by looking at repeated edge weights. Format as markdown with monospaced trace tables. Consider these constraints: break edge-weight ties by lexicographic vertex order (u, v), follow GATE and CLRS conventions, and show union-find operations explicitly. Graph:

prompts.title: Build Study Plan For GATE CSE 2027
prompts.prompt: Act as a GATE CSE mentor with 10 years of experience coaching Indian CS students who have achieved top-1000 ranks and admissions to IIT M.Tech and PSU jobs (BHEL, IOCL, ONGC, PGCIL). I need you to build a realistic 12-month GATE CSE preparation plan. Here is the context: I am a 3rd-year CS student at a Tier-2 college with a current CGPA of 7.5, reasonable programming skills, weak in Mathematics (Discrete Math, Linear Algebra, Probability), and targeting GATE CSE 2027 with a goal rank under 1000. I can study 4 hours on weekdays and 8 hours on weekends, with 2 months full-time after graduation if needed. Please provide: (1) Month-by-month topic breakdown across all 11 sections (Engineering Math, Discrete Math, Digital Logic, Computer Organization, Programming and DS, Algorithms, TOC, Compiler Design, OS, DBMS, Computer Networks) with exact weightage from PYQ analysis, (2) Recommended resources per subject (NPTEL courses by specific IIT professors, standard books like Forouzan, Korth, Galvin, Silberschatz, and YouTube channels like Gate Smashers and Knowledge Gate), (3) When to start solving PYQs (GO Book, GO PDF, Made Easy workbook) and how to use them, (4) Mock test cadence: when to start, how many per week, which platforms (GO Classes, Testbook, Unacademy), (5) Revision strategy for the last 2 months, (6) How to balance GATE prep with college semester exams and placements, (7) A daily 4-hour and weekend 8-hour template, (8) Red flags that indicate I am falling behind and how to course-correct, (9) Mental-health and burnout-avoidance tips specific to 12-month prep. Format as a markdown table for the month-wise plan. Consider these constraints: no coaching budget for offline classes, rely on free and low-cost resources, and the plan must be sustainable alongside college.

prompts.title: Low Level Design Parking Lot
prompts.prompt: Act as a senior Java engineer with 10 years of experience at Uber and Walmart India who conducts Low-Level Design rounds for SDE-2 and SDE-3 candidates. I need you to design a parking lot system as asked in real LLD interviews. Here is the context: I am a final-year M.Tech student at IIIT Hyderabad preparing for LLD rounds where the interviewer expects UML-level class design, SOLID principles, and working code in Java or C++. Please provide: (1) A 5-minute requirement clarification the candidate should ask (types of vehicles, number of floors, pricing by duration, entry-exit flow, multiple gates, handicap spots, electric charging) and my assumed answers, (2) Identification of entities: ParkingLot, ParkingFloor, ParkingSpot (with subtypes), Vehicle (with subtypes Car, Bike, Truck), Ticket, Payment, Gate, DisplayBoard, (3) A class diagram described in text with attributes, methods, and relationships (association, aggregation, composition, inheritance), (4) How each SOLID principle is applied: Single Responsibility (Ticket vs Payment classes), Open-Closed (adding new vehicle types), Liskov (Vehicle subclasses), Interface Segregation (IPaymentStrategy), Dependency Inversion (ParkingLot depends on interfaces), (5) Design patterns used: Strategy for pricing (hourly, flat, subscription), Factory for spot allocation, Singleton for ParkingLot, Observer for DisplayBoard, (6) Java 17 code for the key classes: ParkingLot, ParkingSpot, Vehicle, Ticket, PricingStrategy interface with two implementations, (7) The core algorithm for finding the nearest spot for a given vehicle type, (8) How concurrency is handled when two entry gates assign the same spot, (9) Sample driver code showing park-unpark flow with INR pricing, (10) 3 follow-up questions and crisp answers. Format as markdown with fenced java code blocks. Consider these constraints: prioritize clean abstractions over clever code, follow Java conventions, and be interview-demo ready.

prompts.title: Security Basics OWASP Top 10 For Web Projects
prompts.prompt: Act as an application security engineer with 8 years of experience at an Indian fintech (Razorpay or Juspay) who trains CS students on secure coding before their internships. I need you to audit my college web project against the OWASP Top 10 and give fixes. Here is the context: I am a final-year CS student with a MERN-stack project deployed to a demo server for my final-year viva. I have never thought about security beyond 'hashing passwords'. I will paste my main authentication route, one database query, one file-upload handler, and my CORS/session config right after this prompt. Please provide: (1) A quick summary of the OWASP Top 10 2021 with 1-line definitions, (2) For each item, whether my pasted code is vulnerable, with the exact line flagged, (3) The attack payload an attacker would use (e.g., SQL injection string for MongoDB via $ne operator, XSS payload, JWT none-algorithm attack, path traversal in file upload), (4) The fix with corrected code snippets in Node/Express (parameterized queries with Mongoose, helmet middleware, proper CORS whitelist not *, bcrypt with work factor 12, JWT with refresh tokens and rotation, file-type validation with magic bytes not just extension, rate limiting on login), (5) A checklist of 15 security things to verify before my viva demo, (6) Environment variable hygiene (.env not in git, different secrets for dev/prod), (7) How to explain the security of my project to examiners in 2 minutes using STRIDE threat modeling, (8) 5 free tools to scan my project: npm audit, OWASP ZAP, Snyk, gitleaks, ESLint security plugin, (9) Indian context: IT Rules 2021 and DPDP Act 2023 basics every CS grad should know. Format as markdown with fenced javascript code blocks and a before/after diff per fix. Consider these constraints: fixes must work in a college-demo environment without paid tools, keep explanations accessible to an undergrad, and focus on high-impact vulnerabilities first. Code:
